tension_summary: Hire for raw talent vs. culture fit first

guest_a.name: Shreyas Doshi
guest_a.position: Hire the most talented person you can find, even if they're a poor culture fit — culture can be learned, talent cannot.
guest_a.quote: I'd take a brilliant jerk over a mediocre team player nine times out of ten in a startup context. Talent is the scarcest resource.
guest_a.episode_title: The four types of product work

guest_b.name: Claire Vo
guest_b.position: Culture fit should be a hard filter before evaluating talent, because one bad hire can poison a team.
guest_b.quote: Skills are trainable. Attitude is not. And a team that doesn't trust each other ships nothing.

tension_summary: Ship fast and iterate vs. invest in deep upfront thinking

guest_a.name: Lenny Rachitsky
guest_a.position: The best teams ship fast, learn from users, and course-correct — not spend months planning the perfect roadmap.
guest_a.quote: Your first roadmap is almost always wrong. The faster you can invalidate it, the better.
guest_a.episode_title: How the best PMs do roadmapping

guest_b.name: Marty Cagan
guest_b.position: Shipping fast without deep discovery just means shipping the wrong thing faster — discovery work is non-negotiable.
guest_b.quote: The problem isn't execution speed. Most teams are building the wrong thing efficiently. Discovery is where the real work happens.

tension_summary: Paid acquisition is a trap vs. fastest path to scale

guest_a.name: Andrew Chen
guest_a.position: Paid acquisition is often a crutch that masks weak product-market fit and destroys unit economics at scale.
guest_a.quote: If you need to keep paying to acquire users, you haven't actually built something people want badly enough.

guest_b.name: Brian Balfour
guest_b.position: Paid acquisition, done right, is the highest-leverage growth lever for most B2C products — the key is modeling your LTV/CAC correctly.
guest_b.quote: Dismissing paid because of bad unit economics is like dismissing hiring because a bad hire is expensive. Get your model right first.

tension_summary: NPS is a vanity metric vs. best predictor of growth

guest_a.name: Fareed Mosavat
guest_a.position: NPS is a lagging vanity metric that tells you almost nothing actionable — focus on retention cohorts and feature engagement instead.
guest_a.quote: By the time NPS drops, the damage is already done. It's not a leading indicator of anything useful.

guest_b.name: Rahul Vohra
guest_b.position: NPS, measured correctly and tracked over time, is the single best predictor of long-term retention and organic growth.
guest_b.quote: Companies that obsess over NPS consistently outgrow competitors. It's not the metric, it's how you use it.

tension_summary: Autonomous squads vs. strong central coordination

guest_a.name: Camille Fournier
guest_a.position: Autonomous, empowered squads with end-to-end ownership outperform centrally coordinated teams at every scale.
guest_a.quote: Coordination overhead is the hidden killer of velocity. The more you can push decision-making to the team level, the faster you move.

guest_b.name: Julie Zhuo
guest_b.position: Without strong cross-team coordination and shared context, autonomous squads optimize locally and destroy coherent user experiences.
guest_b.quote: Everyone rowing independently doesn't get you to the same destination faster — you end up with ten boats going in ten directions.

tension_summary: Be decisive and directive vs. lead through questions

guest_a.name: Gokul Rajaram
guest_a.position: Great leaders make crisp, fast decisions and give their teams clarity — ambiguity and endless process are the enemies of execution.
guest_a.quote: The cost of a wrong decision is almost always lower than the cost of no decision. Leaders who hedge lose their teams.

guest_b.name: Lara Hogan
guest_b.position: The best leaders resist the urge to provide answers — asking the right questions builds team capability and surfaces better solutions.
guest_b.quote: Every time you give the answer, you've stolen a learning opportunity. The leader's job is to make their team smarter, not to be the smartest person in the room.

tension_summary: Raise as much as possible vs. stay lean to preserve focus

guest_a.name: Elad Gil
guest_a.position: In uncertain markets, raise as much capital as you can when the window is open — runway is the most valuable asset a startup has.
guest_a.quote: You're not being disciplined by raising less — you're being naive. Capital markets close fast and stay closed for years.

guest_b.name: David Rusenko
guest_b.position: Raising too much capital is one of the most common ways startups kill themselves — it removes urgency, inflates teams, and delays hard decisions.
guest_b.quote: Constraints breed creativity. The startups I've seen fail from too much money far outnumber those that failed from too little.

tension_summary: AI replaces PMs vs. AI makes PMs irreplaceable

guest_a.name: Sachin Rekhi
guest_a.position: AI will fundamentally replace the execution layer of product management — PMs who don't adapt will find their roles eliminated within a decade.
guest_a.quote: The PM job has always been about coordination and synthesis. AI does both faster and cheaper. The question is whether there's anything left after that.

guest_b.name: Gibson Biddle
guest_b.position: AI makes the strategic and empathetic parts of product management more valuable, not less — judgment and taste are the moat.
guest_b.quote: AI can surface patterns. It cannot tell you which patterns matter to real humans or make the moral judgment calls that products require at scale.
guest_b.episode_title: Product strategy in the AI era

tension_summary: Culture is built top-down vs. it emerges from the team

guest_a.name: Ben Horowitz
guest_a.position: Culture is primarily set by the founder and must be deliberately codified and enforced — it doesn't emerge organically at scale.
guest_a.quote: If you don't define the culture, someone else will, and you won't like what you get. This is too important to leave to chance.
guest_a.episode_title: What you do is who you are

guest_b.name: Kim Scott
guest_b.position: Imposed cultures are almost always bullshit — authentic culture emerges from the real behaviors leaders model daily, not from values posters.
guest_b.quote: The moment culture becomes a program, it stops being culture. People are too smart to believe what they're told if it doesn't match what they see.

tension_summary: Quantitative scoring models vs. gut-driven conviction

guest_a.name: Teresa Torres
guest_a.position: Quantitative prioritization frameworks remove bias and force teams to be explicit about their assumptions — gut instinct is just bias with good PR.
guest_a.quote: RICE, ICE, impact-effort matrices — these aren't perfect, but they make your reasoning legible and improve over time. Gut calls don't.

guest_b.name: Paul Adams
guest_b.position: Over-reliance on scoring frameworks creates false precision and penalizes bold bets — the best product decisions are driven by conviction, not spreadsheets.
guest_b.quote: The most important product bets can't be scored in advance. If they could, your competitors would be making them too.

tension_summary: Quantitative data over qualitative vs. user interviews first

guest_a.name: Avinash Kaushik
guest_a.position: Qualitative research is confirmatory theater — if you don't have statistical significance, you're just collecting anecdotes that confirm what you already believe.
guest_a.quote: Five user interviews don't tell you what your product does. They tell you what five articulate people, who agreed to talk to you, think they do.

guest_b.name: Indi Young
guest_b.position: Quantitative data tells you what happened — only qualitative research tells you why, and why is the only thing worth acting on.
guest_b.quote: You can A/B test your way to local maxima forever. Understanding intent is what gets you to the next mountain range entirely.

tension_summary: Fire fast when it's not working vs. invest heavily in coaching first

guest_a.name: Keith Rabois
guest_a.position: The biggest mistake managers make is waiting too long to fire — most people know within weeks whether someone will work out.
guest_a.quote: If you're asking 'should I fire this person,' the answer is almost always yes. The question answered itself.
guest_a.episode_title: The anatomy of a great executive

guest_b.name: Patty McCord
guest_b.position: Most performance failures are organizational or contextual, not individual — firing without investing in coaching is lazy management.
guest_b.quote: Nine out of ten times, when someone is underperforming, the manager hasn't been clear about what success looks like. That's a leadership problem, not a people problem.
guest_b.episode_title: Powerful: building a culture of freedom
